posts.name: Wil Raley
posts.cooked:
I’ve often found Roumier wines to be quite inaccessible young, but late last year I had the opportunity to taste through the 22s and found most of the range to be quite accessible. I think the wines are starting to present a bit differently than they once did with the warming climate. Christophe historically delays malo by cooling the cellar (as apposed to Rousseau who prefers a quick speedy malo) but he failed to delay malo in both 22 and 23 and the wines seemed much more accessible, and candidly better for it.

I had the chance to taste through the range with Christophe (-Musigny of course) last November, and was particularly surprised by the Cras. The structure was much less aggressive and more elegant than I expected. It was all beautiful bright red fruit. There was a clear family resemblance between the Village, Cras and Amoureuses and the Bonnes Mares was also a great wine, but much more purple fruited showing a little more Morey in style. The wines seemed closer to Mugneret Gibourg in style than traditional Roumier. I’d be curious to get other takes on 22 Roumier.

image1920×1651 234 KB

posts.name: Wil Raley
posts.cooked:
I’m not entirely sure what impact it has on structure TBH. Christophe does it to preserve aromatics from what I understand. I know delaying malo is also something that modern Dujac began practicing during the very end of the Jacques Seyess era (influenced by Christophe).

I was blown away by the accessibility of these wines and the malo comment is something I gathered from reading @William_Kelley notes on Roumier’s 22s after tasting through the wines. I’d definitely be curious to hear him weigh in.

posts.name: Marcus Goodfellow
posts.cooked:
In general delaying malo allows an oxidative period between the two reductive phases of ferment and malolactic. Getting a small oxidative phase helps to preserve fruit denseness and freshness (anecdotally) and @Ben_M_a_n_d_l_e_r may be able to explain the chemistry specifically.

It’s important to remember that the oxidative phase is in barrel, so a very small amount of oxygen and is flanked between reductive phases.

That said, the preservation of fruit often makes the wine seem more accessible, which is opposite what Wil is saying. But for a very warm vintage like 2022, finishing secondary ferment early allows the wine to begin knitting together and settling in 4-5 months faster and with plenty of fruit, the wine would show well early.

IMO, for a producer using whole cluster delaying is a good idea. But in a warmer world, that isn’t always easy.

posts.name: Craig Williams
posts.cooked: Wil, when I read your less aggressive qualities in the wine, I thought of phenolics not fragrance and flavor, often bound to glucose and that benefit from longer cellaring on lees. The polymerization of color and tannins in red wine is positively facilitated by O2 and temperature. When red wine color/pigment is at its highest concentration, just after primary fermentation, O2 reactivity is also at its highest, creating a tannin-pigment polymer with greater stability and texture. This reaction is very temp. dependent: research shows O2 is taken up 5 times faster @ 59F than @ 50F. Warmer growing seasons, slightly higher pH and lower malic acid content in grapes often accelerate MLF soon after- 1-3 months- fermentation is complete. And, in my opinion, making the wine more accessible.

posts.name: Wil Raley
posts.cooked: That’s great insight. Thanks for the input Craig!

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: The earlier malolactic thesis was the one I advanced when I reviewed 2022s, and I think it holds true. In some vintages Christophe was bottling only a month after the end of (late) malo. So the wine was going to bottle having only just completed an important microbiological transformation, and having been freshly sulfited. I think that tended to make such vintages show especially primary and structured. Wines that have gone through an earlier malolactic have seen more “real” élevage and taste more resolved and integrated.

posts.name: Trevor Chlanda
posts.cooked:
That and a wine that has just undergone primary fermentation has a significant quantity of dissolved CO2 which acts as a barrier to oxygen ingress - by outcompeting it for binding sites, it outcompetes it and effectively acts as an antioxidant. It protects the wine from oxidation - which is where you see evolution of color and tannin (per @Craig_Williams argument above). If you delay malo, that CO2 will hold for a significant period of time, then when ML begins, you’ll have a fresh level of CO2 in the barrel. If it starts and completes early, you have an evolution of tannin with oxygen ingress in barel as CO2 begins to dissipate earlier that you wouldn’t see with delayed malo. Until that CO2 begins to really subside, you don’t see as big of an oxidative phase. I purposely don’t sulfur wines after malo until I see VA begin to creep to get an evolution of tannin and stability of color.

One other thing that bears mentioning, is the pH shift occurs during malolactic. If you delay, you do keep it slightly lower - usually a 0.1-0.2 shift post, depending on amount of malic acid. As most reactions are temperature and/or pH dependent, it’s not shocking to say this would have an effect on tannin development and “openness” of a wine. Could go into a whole treatise on the development of tannin in barrel and bottle - that’s another discussion.
